threats.title: GaugeController.remove_gauge Always Reverts Due to Pre-Zeroing gauge_types_ Before _remove_gauge_weight Reads It
threats.hypothesis_description: In remove_gauge(), gauge_types_[_gauge] is set to 0 BEFORE _remove_gauge_weight(_gauge) is called. Inside _remove_gauge_weight, gauge_type = gauge_types_[_gauge] - 1 underflows to -1. _get_sum(-1) returns 0 (time_sum[-1] is uninitialized). Then new_sum = 0 - old_weight_bias causes a uint256 underflow revert in Solidity 0.8.x for any gauge with positive weight. Governance permanently loses the ability to remove gauges from the system.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a deterministic code defect that makes a critical governance function non-functional. Any gauge with active votes cannot be removed, meaning malicious or deprecated gauges permanently consume emission weight.

proof_of_signal.pattern: gauge_types_[_gauge] = 0 executed at line 164 before _remove_gauge_weight(_gauge) at line 165; _remove_gauge_weight reads gauge_types_[_gauge] - 1 at its first line, getting -1

preconditions: A gauge exists with gauge_types_[gauge] > 0
The gauge has non-zero weight (any votes allocated)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: gauge_types_ zeroed before _remove_gauge_weight reads it, creating type -1 lookup
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Governance calling remove_gauge will always revert for any gauge with positive weight

operational_questions: Can governance work around this by first zeroing gauge weight via change_gauge_weight, then calling remove_gauge?
Does remove_gauge_weight (separate function) also suffer from this if called on a removed gauge?
validation_requirements: Deploy contracts, add gauge, vote for it, attempt remove_gauge - confirm revert
Test remove_gauge on zero-weight gauge to see if it succeeds but corrupts type -1 data

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Any user votes for a gauge, giving it non-zero weight
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Governance attempts to remove the gauge via remove_gauge()
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Transaction reverts due to uint256 underflow in _remove_gauge_weight
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Gauge remains active permanently, continuing to receive emissions
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - this is a deterministic code bug

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Governance cannot remove malicious or deprecated gauges
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Deprecated gauges permanently dilute emissions for all active gauges

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Any governance call to remove_gauge reverting
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Gauges that should have been removed still receiving emissions

threats.title: Permanent Voting Power Lock When Users Try to Reclaim Votes from Removed Gauges
threats.hypothesis_description: vote_for_gauge_weights allows voting 0 on removed gauges (first require passes when _user_weight==0). However, the subsequent gauge_type = gauge_types_[_gauge_addr] - 1 = 0 - 1 = -1, and require(gauge_type >= 0) reverts. Users who allocated voting power to a gauge before its removal can NEVER reclaim that power. Their vote_user_power permanently includes the trapped allocation, reducing their effective voting capacity forever.
threats.investigation_rationale: This creates a permanent, unrecoverable loss of voting power for affected users. Combined with OPS-H001 making gauge removal broken, even if removal were fixed, users would still lose their power.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Line 204 allows _user_weight==0 on removed gauges, but line 213-214 computes gauge_type = gauge_types_[addr]-1 = -1 and requires gauge_type >= 0, causing revert

preconditions: User has active vote_user_slopes[user][gauge].power > 0 for a gauge
Gauge is removed (gauge_types_[gauge] set to 0)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: First require explicitly allows 0-weight votes on non-gauges, but second require blocks the same code path
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Gauge removal triggers permanent user impact with no recovery mechanism

operational_questions: How many users could be affected if a popular gauge is removed?
Is there any governance function to manually reset vote_user_power?
Can the old vote's slope changes still influence gauge weights after removal?
validation_requirements: Create lock, vote for gauge, remove gauge, attempt vote_for_gauge_weights(gauge, 0) - confirm revert
Verify vote_user_power remains elevated after failed reclaim

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Users vote for gauge G with significant power allocation
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Governance removes gauge G (if removal is fixed or via direct storage)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Users attempt vote_for_gauge_weights(G, 0) to reclaim power - reverts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Users' effective voting capacity is permanently reduced
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: None - affects all voters of the removed gauge

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Users lose governance participation capacity permanently
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Progressive degradation of voting system as more gauges are removed over protocol lifetime
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No on-chain recovery mechanism exists; requires contract redeployment
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All future gauge voting by affected users

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: None for being affected; any veCANTO holder who voted is impacted

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Users with vote_user_power > sum of active vote allocations

threats.title: Retroactive Gauge Weight Manipulation via Fixed epochTime in update_market Loop
threats.hypothesis_description: In update_market, epochTime is computed using block.number (current block) but used for ALL historical block iterations in the while loop. This means gauge_relative_weight_write is called with the SAME current-period time for all past epochs. An attacker can vote heavily for a gauge NOW, then trigger update_market for a market that hasn't been updated in many epochs, retroactively applying the inflated weight to all historical epochs and claiming excess rewards.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a direct economic exploit: the attacker steals CANTO rewards that should have been distributed based on historical weights, at the expense of other markets' depositors.

proof_of_signal.pattern: epochTime = referenceBlockTime + ((block.number - referenceBlockNumber) * averageBlockTime) / 1000 uses block.number (current) inside while loop iterating i from lastRewardBlock to block.number; should use i instead of block.number

preconditions: Market hasn't had update_market called for multiple epochs
Attacker has veCANTO voting power to influence gauge weights
cantoPerBlock is non-zero for the affected epochs
Attacker is a depositor in the target market

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: epochTime uses block.number instead of loop variable i, applying current gauge weight to all historical epochs
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - fully exploitable by unprivileged users
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time a market has stale lastRewardBlock

operational_questions: How frequently is update_market called for each market in practice?
What is the maximum reward inflation achievable per stale epoch?
Can the attacker prevent update_market from being called to build up stale epochs?
validation_requirements: Deploy system, advance multiple epochs without calling update_market, change gauge weights, then call update_market and verify rewards use current weight for all past epochs
Quantify reward inflation vs legitimate allocation

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deposit into market M, ensure no calls to update_market for several epochs
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Vote heavily for M's gauge in GaugeController to maximize relative weight
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call claim(M) which triggers update_market - all stale epochs computed with inflated weight

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Reward distribution systematically favors manipulative depositors
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Cannot undo retroactive reward miscalculation; requires code fix and redeployment
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All reward calculations across all markets

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: Sufficient veCANTO to materially shift gauge weight
execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Moderate - understanding of gauge weight timing
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Exploit window grows with each epoch without update_market call

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Markets with large gaps between lastRewardBlock and current block
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Sudden gauge weight changes followed by claim transactions
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: User voting heavily for a gauge and immediately claiming from the associated market

threats.title: First-Depositor uint128 Overflow DoS Permanently Bricks Market via secRewardsPerShare
threats.hypothesis_description: In update_market, secRewardsPerShare is accumulated as uint128((blockDelta * 1e36) / marketSupply). With marketSupply = 1 (1 wei deposit), a single epoch iteration with blockDelta >= 340 causes the value to exceed uint128 max (~3.4e38). The uint128() cast in Solidity 0.8.17 reverts on overflow. Once this state is reached, ALL calls to update_market, sync_ledger, claim, and LiquidityGauge operations permanently revert, bricking the market and trapping all deposited funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: Any attacker can be the first depositor with 1 wei in a newly whitelisted market. The secRewardsPerShare overflow is independent of cantoPerBlock settings - it occurs automatically over ~340 blocks (~32 minutes).

proof_of_signal.pattern: market.secRewardsPerShare += uint128((blockDelta * 1e36) / marketSupply); with marketSupply=1 and blockDelta=340, value=3.4e38 exceeds uint128 max causing revert

preconditions: Market is newly whitelisted with no prior deposits
Attacker is first to deposit (can deposit 1 wei)
~340 blocks pass before another significant deposit

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: uint128 cast on value that grows linearly with blockDelta and inversely with marketSupply
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - any user can be first depositor
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: ~340 blocks (~32 minutes at 5.7s block time) after first 1-wei deposit

operational_questions: Does Solidity 0.8.17 revert on uint128() narrowing cast overflow?
Can a larger second deposit before overflow prevent the attack?
Is there any mechanism to reset lastRewardBlock or secRewardsPerShare?
validation_requirements: Deploy system, whitelist market, deposit 1 wei, advance 400 blocks, attempt update_market - confirm revert
Test with accCantoPerShare overflow path when cantoPerBlock > 0

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor governance for new market whitelisting transactions
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Market is permanently bricked - all operations revert on update_market

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Newly whitelisted market permanently non-functional; user funds trapped in LiquidityGauge
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No on-chain recovery; requires redeployment and migration
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All deposit, withdrawal, and claim operations for the bricked market

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Must be first depositor after whitelist

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Markets where all operations revert after initial deposit
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Tiny deposits from unknown addresses immediately after whitelisting

threats.title: LiquidityGauge ERC20Burnable Exposes Irreversible Fund Loss via Public burn() and burnFrom()
threats.hypothesis_description: LiquidityGauge inherits ERC20Burnable, exposing public burn(uint256) and burnFrom(address,uint256). Calling burn destroys gauge tokens and triggers _afterTokenTransfer → sync_ledger (reducing user's ledger balance), but does NOT return the underlying tokens. The underlying remains permanently stuck in the gauge contract with no recovery mechanism. Any user or approved contract calling burn instead of withdrawUnderlying permanently loses their deposited principal.
threats.investigation_rationale: This violates the stated invariant that gauge tokens are backed 1:1 by underlying. burn() creates a permanent divergence between totalSupply and underlying balance, and the burned user's tokens are irrecoverable.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Inherits ERC20Burnable but never overrides burn/burnFrom; _afterTokenTransfer calls sync_ledger for burns but withdrawUnderlying is the only function that transfers underlying back

preconditions: User holds LiquidityGauge tokens
User or approved contract calls burn() or burnFrom()

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: ERC20Burnable inherited unnecessarily; burn destroys tokens without returning underlying
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - any token holder can burn

operational_questions: Are any integrating contracts expected to call burn()?
Can stuck underlying tokens be recovered by governance?
Does the documentation warn users about burn() vs withdrawUnderlying()?
validation_requirements: Deposit underlying, call burn(), verify underlying stuck in gauge, verify ledger balance reduced

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User deposits underlying tokens via depositUnderlying, receiving gauge tokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: User (or approved contract) calls burn(amount) instead of withdrawUnderlying(amount)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Gauge tokens destroyed, ledger balance reduced, underlying remains locked in gauge
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: No recovery mechanism exists - funds permanently lost

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Individual user fund loss; remaining gauge tokens are still fully backed
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Impossible - no function to extract orphaned underlying
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Any user/contract interaction using ERC20Burnable interface

threats.title: De-Whitelisting Market Permanently Traps All User Funds in LiquidityGauge
threats.hypothesis_description: When governance sets lendingMarketWhitelist[market] = false, update_market reverts with 'Market not whitelisted'. LiquidityGauge._afterTokenTransfer calls sync_ledger on every transfer/mint/burn, and sync_ledger calls update_market. This means ALL LiquidityGauge operations (withdrawUnderlying, transfer, transferFrom, burn, burnFrom) revert. Users' underlying tokens are permanently trapped in the gauge contract with no escape mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a critical code design flaw: the whitelist check gates both reward accrual AND basic token operations. Governance legitimately wanting to stop emissions for a market also permanently locks all user funds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: update_market requires lendingMarketWhitelist[_market]; sync_ledger always calls update_market; _afterTokenTransfer always calls sync_ledger; no bypass exists

preconditions: Market has been whitelisted with a LiquidityGauge
Users have deposited underlying tokens via the gauge
Governance de-whitelists the market

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Whitelist check in update_market blocks ALL gauge operations, not just reward accrual
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires governance to de-whitelist, but this is a legitimate operational action
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Immediate upon de-whitelisting and permanent until re-whitelisting

operational_questions: Can governance re-whitelist to allow withdrawals, then immediately de-whitelist again?
Is there any direct recovery mechanism for trapped underlying?
Are there plans to de-whitelist markets in normal operations?
validation_requirements: Whitelist market with gauge, deposit, de-whitelist, attempt withdrawUnderlying - confirm revert
Confirm ALL gauge operations (transfer, burn, etc.) also revert

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Users deposit underlying tokens into gauge for a whitelisted market
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Governance de-whitelists the market (legitimate operational action or malicious)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: All LiquidityGauge operations revert due to update_market whitelist check
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: User funds permanently trapped until governance re-whitelists

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Governance can hold user funds hostage by de-whitelisting
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All deposited user funds in the gauge permanently locked
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires governance to re-whitelist (if they're willing/able)
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All deposit, withdrawal, and transfer operations for affected gauge

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time after users have deposited

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: whiteListLendingMarket called with false for markets with non-zero gauge balances

threats.title: unlockOverride Enables Cross-Address Voting Power Amplification in GaugeController
threats.hypothesis_description: When unlockOverride is enabled, users can withdraw locked CANTO early. However, GaugeController is not notified of the withdrawal - the user's allocated gauge votes (vote_user_slopes) remain active until the original 5-year lock_end. An attacker can: lock CANTO → vote for gauge A → withdraw early → transfer CANTO to address B → lock again at B → vote for gauge B. Both votes remain active simultaneously, amplifying total gauge influence from a single CANTO position.
threats.investigation_rationale: The VotingEscrow and GaugeController have no integration for withdrawal notifications. When unlockOverride makes early withdrawals possible, the assumption that locks persist until lock_end is violated, but GaugeController still relies on it.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdraw() zeroes lock and checkpoints VotingEscrow, but has no callback to GaugeController; vote_user_slopes[user][gauge] persists with original slope and lock_end

preconditions: unlockOverride is enabled by governance
Attacker has CANTO to lock
Attacker has voted for gauges before withdrawing

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: VotingEscrow.withdraw has no integration point with GaugeController to clear votes
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires unlockOverride to be enabled (governance action)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Active for the entire duration unlockOverride is enabled

operational_questions: Does the decaying slope from the original lock still produce meaningful gauge weight?
Can the attacker cycle CANTO through N addresses for N-fold amplification?
What is the actual economic gain from amplified gauge weight?
validation_requirements: Enable unlockOverride, lock and vote at address A, withdraw and re-lock at address B, vote at B, verify both gauge votes are active simultaneously

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Lock CANTO at address A, vote 10000 bps for gauge X
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Governance enables unlockOverride for legitimate reasons
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Withdraw CANTO from A, transfer to address B
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Lock at B, vote 10000 bps for gauge Y (or same gauge X)
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Attacker controls multiple addresses, needs unlockOverride enabled

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Gauge weight distribution corrupted; emissions misdirected
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Phantom votes decay over ~5 years; no immediate fix
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All gauge weight calculations and reward distribution

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: Low - single attacker with multiple addresses

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Withdrawals during unlockOverride followed by new locks from same owner pattern
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Gauge weights exceeding expected totals based on locked CANTO

threats.title: LendingLedger whiteListLendingMarket Sets lastRewardBlock on Wrong Key for Gauge-Enabled Markets
threats.hypothesis_description: When whiteListLendingMarket is called with _hasGauge=true, the function sets marketInfo[_market].lastRewardBlock = block.number. However, when the LiquidityGauge calls sync_ledger, msg.sender is the gauge address, and liquidityGauges[gauge] = _market, so lendingMarket resolves to _market. update_market(_market) uses marketInfo[_market].lastRewardBlock which WAS correctly set. However, if whiteListLendingMarket is called AFTER the gauge is already created (second call with _isWhiteListed change), the market key IS _market. The real issue: if a market is de-whitelisted then re-whitelisted, lastRewardBlock is set to current block.number, but accCantoPerShare/secRewardsPerShare retain their old accumulated values, creating a desynchronization between the reward accumulator state and the block tracking.
threats.investigation_rationale: Re-whitelisting resets the block counter but not the accumulated reward per share, potentially enabling reward extraction for the gap period or creating accounting inconsistencies.

proof_of_signal.pattern: lastRewardBlock reset to block.number on re-whitelist but accCantoPerShare/secRewardsPerShare NOT reset; gap between old lastRewardBlock and new one is skipped

preconditions: Market was previously whitelisted with accumulated rewards
Governance de-whitelists then re-whitelists the market
Users had deposits during the de-whitelisted period

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Re-whitelisting skips reward accumulation for gap period; existing users' rewardDebt is based on old accCantoPerShare; mismatch may allow or prevent claims
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires governance to de-whitelist and re-whitelist
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During re-whitelisting of previously active markets

operational_questions: Do existing users get free rewards or lose rewards when re-whitelisted?
Can a new depositor during re-whitelist extract rewards from the skipped gap?
Does the accounting remain consistent for all users?
validation_requirements: Whitelist market, deposit, accumulate rewards, de-whitelist, re-whitelist, verify claim behavior for old depositors vs new depositors

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Market is whitelisted and has active depositors with accumulated rewards
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Governance de-whitelists market (OPS-H006 applies - funds trapped)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Governance re-whitelists market to unblock funds
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: New depositors entering after re-whitelist may have rewardDebt = 0 but accCantoPerShare is high from historical accumulation, allowing immediate claim of phantom rewards

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Reward accounting desynchronized; phantom rewards claimable or legitimate rewards lost
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Cannot repair accumulated reward state without redeployment
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All claim operations for the re-whitelisted market

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: whiteListLendingMarket toggling for same market address
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: New deposits immediately after re-whitelist followed by immediate claims

threats.title: VotingEscrow Checkpoint Loop Iteration Limit (255) Insufficient for Lock Duration (260 Weeks)
threats.hypothesis_description: The _checkpoint function iterates at most 255 weeks to fill global point history. LOCKTIME is 1825 days ≈ 260.7 weeks. If no checkpoint is called for >255 weeks, the remaining ~5 weeks of slope changes go unprocessed, causing incorrect globalEpoch, totalSupply, and voting power calculations. The _supplyAt function has the same 255-iteration limit. This creates a 5-week window where slope changes are missed.
threats.investigation_rationale: While the protocol documentation states checkpoint must be called within 5 years, the code's 255-week limit is strictly less than the 260-week lock duration, meaning even if called exactly at the boundary, some slope changes are missed.

proof_of_signal.pattern: for (uint256 i = 0; i < 255; i++) iterates max 255 weeks; LOCKTIME = 1825 days = 260.7 weeks; 255 < 261 means 5+ weeks of slope changes can be missed

preconditions: No checkpoint called for >255 weeks (~4.9 years)
Active locks with slope changes in weeks 256-261

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Loop iteration count does not cover full lock duration
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Only manifests after 255+ weeks without checkpoint

operational_questions: On Canto chain, how likely is 255 weeks without any VE interaction?
What is the quantitative impact of missing 5 weeks of slope changes?
Does _supplyAt have the same limitation for historical queries?
validation_requirements: Create lock, advance 256+ weeks without checkpoint, call checkpoint, verify some slope changes are unprocessed

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Protocol exists for >4.9 years with decreasing activity
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: totalSupply and voting power calculations incorrect
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires extended period without any VE interaction

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Incorrect voting power calculations for ~5 week window
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Unrecoverable - missed slope changes are permanently lost

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Discrepancy between expected and actual totalSupply

threats.title: Missing Delegate/Undelegate Functions Create Dead Code Paths and Incomplete Governance Feature
threats.hypothesis_description: VotingEscrow's LockedBalance struct has delegatee and delegated fields, the LockAction enum defines DELEGATE and UNDELEGATE, and increaseAmount contains an else-branch for delegated locks. However, no delegate() or undelegate() functions exist. The delegatee field is permanently set to msg.sender in createLock and can never be changed. The delegation code paths in increaseAmount can never execute. The withdraw function's require(locked_.delegatee == msg.sender) check is always true but would block withdrawals if delegation were somehow enabled in the future.
threats.investigation_rationale: Dead code and incomplete features indicate an incomplete implementation that may cause issues if the protocol evolves or if users expect delegation functionality based on the code architecture.

proof_of_signal.pattern: LockAction enum includes DELEGATE and UNDELEGATE (lines 66-67); LockedBalance has delegatee/delegated fields (lines 52-57); no delegate()/undelegate() functions exist in the contract

preconditions: Contract deployed as-is without delegation functions

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Enum variants, struct fields, and code branches exist for delegation but no entry points to trigger them
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: No administrative path to enable delegation
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: N/A - missing functionality rather than active exploit

operational_questions: Was delegation intentionally removed or accidentally omitted?
Does the increaseAmount delegation branch have bugs that would manifest if delegation existed?
Could a future upgrade accidentally enable delegation in an inconsistent state?
validation_requirements: Verify delegatee is always msg.sender for all lock states
Verify delegation code path in increaseAmount is unreachable

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: N/A - this is a missing functionality finding

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users cannot delegate voting power as architecture implies they should
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires new contract deployment with delegation functions

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: All locked balances have delegatee == owner

threats.title: Missing quitLock Function Leaves Users Without Early Exit Mechanism for 5-Year Locks
threats.hypothesis_description: The LockAction enum defines QUIT (implying an early exit with penalty, standard in Curve-derived implementations), but no quitLock() function exists. Users who lock CANTO are committed for 5 years with every action (createLock, increaseAmount) resetting the lock duration. The only escape is the governance-controlled unlockOverride, which is a global all-or-nothing toggle. Users have no individual early exit mechanism, even with a penalty, and governance must risk the voting power amplification bug (OPS-H007) to provide any relief.
threats.investigation_rationale: The absence of an individual early exit mechanism combined with the 5-year lock that resets on every action creates a significant user safety concern. If unlockOverride is the only relief valve, it introduces systemic risk.

proof_of_signal.pattern: LockAction.QUIT defined in enum at line 63 but no quitLock() function exists; withdraw requires lock.end <= block.timestamp || unlockOverride

preconditions: User has an active lock with CANTO
Lock has not expired
unlockOverride is not enabled

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: QUIT enum variant exists without corresponding function; user funds locked for full 5-year duration
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Only governance can enable early withdrawal via unlockOverride

operational_questions: Was quitLock intentionally omitted or accidentally left out?
What penalty rate would be appropriate for early exit?
How does the lack of individual exit affect user adoption?
validation_requirements: Verify no early exit path exists besides unlockOverride
Verify increaseAmount resets lock to full 5 years

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Financial circumstances change, user needs liquidity
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: User must wait full 5 years or lobby governance for unlockOverride

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: User funds locked for extended periods without recourse
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Only governance intervention via unlockOverride (which has side effects per OPS-H007)
